details.text: Derivative: يَلْتَقِطْ comes from اِلْتَقَطَ which means to take or pick up something from an unanticipated place that you just stumble upon. It is like something you find on a path or road. From the same root لَقِيط (Laqeet) was used to refer to a child or person that was picked up and taken whilst لُقْطَة (Luqtah) referred to an object.
Derivative: غَيَابَتِ – غَيَابَة (Ghayaabah) refers to any place that hides something from you and conceals it. It is used to refer to the bottom and depths of a well or dungeon.

Derivative: الْجُبِّ (Jubb) refers to a well or a pit. A جُبّ (Jubb) “well” is less defined and built than a بِئْر (Bi’r) which is a well that has been built with greater structuring. A جُبّ (Jubb) is usually found outside of towns where there is less built-up civilisation like the desert.
Derivative: لَطِيفٌ (Lateef) “to be subtle” which implies that He (Allah) brings to fruition His plan in ways that one cannot perceive.
Derivative: بَصِيرَةٍ (Baseerah) refers to “clear insight and vision”. It implies “clear proof, evidence and certain real knowledge”.
Derivative: نَصْر (Nasr) is used to refer to aid, support and victory that is given against an enemy.
Derivative: عِبْرَةٌ refers to “a lesson”, “warning” and “example” that should be given consideration to and heeded.
Derivative: يَرْتَعْ from رَتَعَ which means “to graze” where cattle freely graze. It is used generally to mean to indulge freely i.e., to go around without restraints and enjoy oneself.
Derivative: أَجْمَعُوا comes from أَجْمَعَ – إِجْماعًا which means “to hold a consensus”, “to be unified and agreed” or “to be collectively resolved” to undertake a matter.

Derivative: الْمُسْتَعَانُ (Al-Musta’aan) is the اِسْمُ المَفْعُول (Ism ul-Maf’ool – Passive participle) and means “The One (alone) whose aid is sought” from اِسْتَعَانَ – يَسْتَعِينُ – اِسْتِعَانَةً.

Derivative: سَوَّلَتْ from سَوَّلَ - تِسْوِيل which means “to entice, persuade or seduce” somebody to do something. It can imply being persuaded or enticed by something to see something bad as being good or no big deal.
Derivative: بِضَاعَةً (Bidaa’ah) refers to goods and merchandise that you are going to sell.
Derivative: بَخْسٍ (Bakhs) refers to a very low price i.e., well under its true value.

Derivative: الزَّاهِدِينَ is the plural زَاهِد (Zaahid) which comes from زًهْد (Zuhd) which means to be not concerned with worldly things. Here الزَّاهِدِينَ implies the meaning of “those who had little care or concern”.

Derivative: مَثْوَى (Mathwaa) comes from ثَوَى – يَثْوِي – ثَواءً which in respect to a place means “to reside and settle there” for a long time. مَثْوَى (Mathwaa) refers to the place in which somebody “resides and settles”

Derivative: أَشُدَّ (Ashudda) refers to “physical maturity” in terms of strength. It comes from شِدَّة (Shiddah) which implies “strength”. With بَلَغَ (to reach) it means to “attain full physical maturity”. There are different opinions regarding how old that is but most opinions range between 18 and 30 years of age.

Derivative: حُكْمًا (Hukm) in origin means “judgement”. It has been stated here to refer to the meaning of حِكْمَة (Hikmah - Wisdom) which implies hitting the mark in action and speech i.e., being able to make the right decision or say the right thing in given situations. The use of حُكْمًا (Hukm) here has also been said to refer to the meaning of نُبُوَّة (Nubuwwah – Prophethood).

Derivative: رَاوَدَتْ comes from رَاوَدَ – يُرَاوِدُ – مُوارَدَة which comes from the root of رود. رَادَ – يَرُودُ – رَوْد means to go about looking or be on the prowl for something. أَرَادَ – يُرِيدُ – إِرَادَة is to “want or intend something”. رَاوَدَ means to attempt to get somebody to have the same want or intention that you have or to entice them into doing something. In this way it is used to mean: “to attempt to seduce or tempt someone”.
Derivative: هَيْتَ (Haita) is an اِسْمُ فِعْلِ الأمْر (Ismu Fi’l il-Amr) which is noun that expresses a verb of command. It carries the meaning of: “Come and hurry”.

Derivative: مَعَاذَ (Ma’aadha) can be a مَصْدَر (Masdar- Verbal noun) which is used with Allah’s name like the statement مَعَاذَ اللَّهِ “Ma’aadhAllah” carrying the meaning of “(I seek) The refuge of Allah” with the “I seek” being implied but not stated. مَعَاذ is also the اِسْم الظَّرْف (Adverbial) form and can refer to “a place of refuge”.
Derivative: غَلَّقَت – أَغْلَقَ (Aghlaqa) means “to lock” something like a door. غَلَّقَ (Ghallaqa) holds greater emphasis and means “to really make sure that something is locked well”.
Derivative: هَمَّ – يَهُمُّ – هَمًّا means to intend or have in mind to do something, or to be on the verge of doing something.

Derivative: الْمُخْلَصِينَ is the plural of مُخْلَص (Mukhlas) which is the اِسْم المَفْعُول (Ism ul-Maf’ool – Passive participle) and means the person who “has been made sincere”. It differs from مُخْلِص (Mukhlis) which is the اِسْمُ الفَاعِل (Ism ul-Faa’il – Active participle) and means the person who “is sincere”.
Derivative: أَلْفَيَا is the dual form from أَلْفَى – يُلْفِي – إِلْفَاء which means “to find” but is used when you find something that you were not looking for and stumble upon it.

Derivative: نَقُصُّ comes from قَصَّ – يَقُصُّ which was used in Arabic to mean to “trace or follow” footsteps or tracks. From this the meaning or “relating” and “narrating” stories which are passed down from one generation to the next was adopted in Arabic. From that قِصَّة (Qissah) became the word used for “a story” and قِصَص (Qisas) is its plural.

Derivative: الْغَافِلِينَ is the plural of الغَافِل (Al-Ghaafil) and is from غَفَلَ – يَغْفُلُ – غَفْلَة which means “to be heedless, unaware and inattentive”. الغَافِل (Al-Ghaafil) can be somebody who is spiritually heedless in terms of not being attentive to Allah and it can also be somebody who is unaware of something and doesn’t know anything about it.
Derivative: شَغَفَ means to infatuate and to fill someone’s heart with strong passion and love.

Derivative: نِسْوَةٌ (Niswah) refers to “women” and differs from نِسَاء (Nisaa’) which means “women in general”. نِسْوَةٌ (Niswah) is used to indicate to a particular group of women and usually in a particular context that defines this group like a common interest or reality.

Derivative: مُتَّكَأً comes from the verb اِتَّكَأَ – يَتَّكِئُ - اِتِّكَاءً which means to lean and is used to refer to sitting by leaning on one of the two sides. مُتَّكَأً (Muttaka’a) is something to lean and recline upon and implies a comfortable place to sit upon.
Derivative: أَكْبَرْنَ is the feminine plural form of أَكْبَرَ which means to consider something to be great, to laud and show great admiration towards it.

Derivative: مَكْر (Makr) is from مَكَرَ – يَمْكُرُ which means to plot and scheme in a precise manner employing clever indirect or covert ploys in order to cause harm to somebody, undermine them or accomplish a particular personal interest.

- حَاشَ لِلَّهِ (Haasha Lillah) is an expression that means “To declare Allah’s perfection and His being free of any flaw and imperfection” attached to the element of astonishment. It is used here as an expression of amazement and astonishment at Allah’s creation in relation to Yusuf (as).
Derivative: الصَّاغِرِينَ is the plural of صَاغِر (Saaghir) which refers to the one who has been made “small” i.e., degraded and humiliated.
Derivative: اسْتَعْصَمَ – يَسْتَعْصِمُ – اِسْتِعْصَام which means to hold on firmly to protect oneself and can imply the meaning of “to firmly resist” something like an urge or temptation.

Derivative: أَصْبُ comes from صَبَا – يَصْبُو – صَبْوَة which means to “incline or bend towards (إِلَى) a desire” i.e., to incline towards giving into a desire. The verb صَبَا is also used to mean “to be a child” and a parallel can be drawn between the impulsive behaviour of a child and giving into one’s desires or impulses.
Derivative: مِلَّةَ (Millah) refers to a belief or belief system that a collective of people follow like a religion.

- “And they (وَهُم), in the hereafter (بِالْآخِرَةِ), they (هُمْ) are disbelievers (كَافِرُونَ)” – This is a very uniquely emphatic sentence structure demonstrated by the repetition of هُمْ (Hum – They). It places great emphasis upon their disbelief in the hereafter and consequently the absence of any fear of accountability for the wrongs that they perpetrate.

إِذْ قَالَ يُوسُفُ لِأَبِيهِ يَا أَبَتِ إِنِّي رَأَيْتُ أَحَدَ عَشَرَ كَوْكَبًا وَالشَّمْسَ وَالْقَمَرَ رَأَيْتُهُمْ لِي سَاجِدِينَ

- In reference to the eleven stars (أَحَدَ عَشَرَ كَوْكَبًا), the sun (الشَّمْسَ) and the moon (الْقَمَر), instead of using the plural form for objects or non-humans, the masculine plural “رَأَيْتُهُمْ” and “سَاجِدِينَ” is used which is reserved for humans or those who possess an ‘Aql (rational thinking mind). This implies that it was known whom the celestial bodies were referring to.
- يَا أَبَتِ (Yaa Abati) “O father” is an expression that implies a greater level of respect and endearment than the usual expression يَا أَبِي (Yaa Abi).
Derivative: بِضْعَ has been said to refer to a number that lies between 3 and 9.
Derivative: عِجَافٌ (‘Ijaaf) is the plural of أَعْجَف (A’ajaf) and it means to be skinny and weak due to not eating.

- “Mixed up and convoluted (أَضْغَاثُ) dreams (أَحْلَامٍ)” – أَضْغَاثُ (Adghaath) is the plural of ضِغْث (Dighth) and was used in Arabic when to describe a bundle or package consisting of mixed-up things. أَضْغَاثُ أَحْلَامٍ (Adghaathu Ahlaam) is an expression to mean dreams that have no meaning or significance i.e., they are just random dreams.
Derivative: أُمَّةٍ (Ummah) – One of the meanings of أُمَّةٍ (Ummah) is “a period of time” and implies a lengthy period of time.
Derivative: حَصْحَصَ means to become clear and plain or to manifest and come to light in relation to the truth: حَصْحَصَ الْحَقُّ.
Derivative: يَتَبَوَّأُ is from تَبَوَّأَ which means to take a place to settle down in.
Derivative: يَجْتَبِي comes from اِجْتَبَى which means to “choose” or “make a selection” based on an evaluation of someone’s qualities and an assessment of the person being qualified.
Derivative: تَأْوِيلِ (Ta’weel) comes from أَوَّلَ – يُؤَوِّلُ which means to “interpret” something which is not clear or confusing.
Derivative: رِحَالِ (Rihaal) is the plural of رَحْل (Rahl) and refers to saddlebags or baggage that you take with you on a journey رِحْلَة (Rihlah).

Derivative: نَمِيرُ comes from مَارَ – يَمِيرُ – مَيْر which means “to provide” in the context of providing for one’s family. مِيرَة (Meerah) refers to “provisions” that are brought from one place to another.

- “Unless (إِلَّا) you are encircled (أَن يُحَاطَ بِكُمْ)” أَحَاطَ – يُحِيطُ – إِحَاطَة means to be surrounded, encompassed or enclosed. With بِ (Bi) it carries the meaning of being closed in from all sides or to be encircled. Here, it can mean this literally in the case where they are surrounded by raiders on their travels or it can carry the meaning of being in circumstances where they have no ability or power to do anything i.e., beyond their capability to prevent something.

Derivative: آوَىٰ – إيواءً means to provide someone with refuge or shelter. It is also used to mean “to bring or draw” someone close. آوَىٰ إِلَيْهِ أَخَاهُ “He brought his brother close to himself” i.e., to his side.

Derivative: الْعِيرُ (‘Eer) is one of the words used to refer to a caravan like قَافِلَة (Qaafilah) or سَيَّارَة (Sayyaarah) i.e., a procession of people with riding animals and baggage or merchandise travelling together.

Derivative: السِّقَايَةَ (Siqaayah) comes from سَقَى which means “to give or provide something to drink” to someone or something. سِقَايَة (Siqaayah) can refer to a “drinking vessel” or to “a bowl or container” that was used to measure out provisions. It has been said that it is precious and valuable.

Derivative: زَعِيمٌ (Za’eem) in relation to carrying something out refers to someone who is “responsible for it and a guarantor of it” (كَفِيل - Kafeel) i.e., someone who can be trusted and has the capability to see something done or a matter carried out.
Derivative: صُوَاعَ (Suwaa’) is used here instead of سِقَايَة (Siqaayah) which was used previously and like it, it can either refer to a drinking vessel or a container used to measure out measures.
- تَاللَّهِ (Tallaahi) like وَاللَّهِ (Wallaahi) means “By Allah” i.e., to swear by Allah. However, تَاللَّهِ (Tallaahi) is used when expressing astonishment (التَّعَجُّب – At-Ta’ajjub).

Derivative: اسْتَخْرَجَ means the process of taking something out of something else and extracting it. اسْتَخْرَجَ – اِسْتِخْرَاج (Istikhraaj) differs from أَخْرَجَ – إِخْرَاج (Ikhraaj) in that the latter is not concerned with the process and is more direct in meaning to “take something out”.
Derivative: دِينِ (Deen) used here in the statement: فِي دِينِ الْمَلِكِ “In the Deen of the king” refers to the “law” of the king.

Derivative: وِعَاءِ (Wi’aa’) means something like a vessel or container that things are gathered inside of and kept safe. Here it refers to the bags which they would travel with, tie to their saddles and keep their things in. أَوْعِيَة (Aw’iyah) is the plural of وِعَاءِ (Wi’aa’).
Derivative: عُصْبَةٌ (Usbah) refers to a group where each of its individuals gives support and strength to the others in it.

Derivative: فَرَّطتُمْ is from فَرَّطَ – تَفْرِيط and it means “to fall short” in respect to one’s responsibilities or obligations and to do less than what was required or expected. It contrasts with أَفْرَطَ – إِفْرَاط from the same Arabic root which means to “exceed the proper limits”, “to be excessive and go too far”.

Derivative: أَبْرَحَ comes from بَرِحَ – يَبْرَحُ which means to leave or depart from a place. It is from the sisters (أَخَوات - Akhawaat) of كَانَ and when preceded by a negation like ما or لَا it is from the verbs that imply constancy i.e., to remain upon a course of action or state and condition. The meaning here of the statement لَنْ أَبْرَحَ is “I will never leave” or “not leave at all”.

Derivative: سَوَّلَتْ from سَوَّلَ - تِسْوِيل which means “to entice, persuade or seduce” somebody to do something. It can imply being persuaded or enticed by something to see something bad as being good or no big deal.

Derivative: كَظِيمٌ (Kazheem) comes from كَظَمَ - يَكْظِمُ – كَظْم which means to “supress and hold back or in” which is used in related to anger or emotional grief when it has reached its upper limit. كَظِيمٌ (Kazheem) refers to the person who is filled with great sorrow and grief but conceals it from the people.
- يَا أَسَفَىٰ “Yaa Asafaa” is an expression used to express one’s feeling of extreme sadness and grief over something that has happened in the past.
Derivative: حَرَضًا (Harad) means to be “so frail or sick” that you no longer have the capability to move or speak i.e., to be on the verge of death due to sickness or extreme anguish.

Derivative: تَفْتَأُ is from فَتَأَ or فَتِئَ – يَفْتَأُ. It is from the sisters (أَخَوات - Akhawaat) of كَانَ and when preceded by a negation like ما or لَا it is from the verbs that imply constancy i.e., to remain upon a course of action or state and condition. فَتَأَ means to “desist”, “refrain” or “cease” from doing something whilst مَا فَتَأَ means to keep doing something or to do it incessantly. Here the meaning is that of negation لَا تَفْتَأ with the meaning of “You keep (i.e., doing something)” but the لَا has been omitted although implicitly understood.
Derivative: بَثِّ (Bathth) refers to extreme grief and sorrow and it is called that because the person is unable to remain patient with it for the purpose of not revealing it to the people.

Derivative: تَحَسَّسُوا is from تَحَسَّسَ which in origin carries the meaning of “to feel around” from حَسّ (Hass) which means “feeling and sensation” and implies to seek and look for something via the use of one’s senses in a careful and deliberate manner. It can imply the meaning of “to probe for information” and to “make inquiries” i.e., engage in fact-finding or information gathering.
Derivative: رَوْح (Rawh) implies the meaning of the “release” or “mercy” of Allah in relation to a difficult situation. It comes from أَرَاحَ (Araaha) which carries the meaning of “to give relief”.
Derivative: مُزْجَاة the feminine of مُزْجًى refers to something that is “little, paltry and insignificant”.

Derivative: يَخْلُ comes from خَلَا – يَخْلُو – خُلُو / خَلَاء / خَلْوَة which carries the meanings of “to be alone, isolate, devote or free”. The meaning in this Aayah is “to be dedicated exclusively” i.e., your father’s attention will only be towards you.

Derivative: اطْرَحُو is the plural command form from طَرَحَ – يَطْرَحُ – طَرْحًا and is used to mean “to cast, toss or fling something aside or out of sight”. It is used in respect top something you hold little or no value for. It is also used to mean “to expel and cast out”.
Derivative: تَثْرِيب comes from ثَرَّبَ – يُثَرِّبُ – تَثْرِيبًا which combines the meanings of blame and reproach.
Derivative: تُفَنِّدُون is from فَنَّدَ – يُفَنِّدُ – تَفْنِيدًا means to declare someone weak of mind due to sickness or being of an advanced age.
Derivative: فَصَلَت – فَصَلَ means to separate, detach and set apart. In respect to travel it means “to depart”.
Derivative: ارْتَدَّ – اِرْتِدَاد means “to return to a previous state or condition”.
